text:
**Filter construction from joined-channels state** ‚Äî `src/fabric/subscriptions.rs` is a **pure planner** (holds no transport handle, `:171`). Three aggregate filter builders: `aggregate_h_filter` (kinds 9/30315/30023 by `#h`, `:74`), `aggregate_p_filter` (9/30023 by `#p`, `:85`), `aggregate_gstate_filter` (39000/39001/39002 by `#d`, `:96`). `SubscriptionRegistry` holds `aggregate` + `narrow` coverage sets; `seed(coverage)` returns the 3 aggregate REQs `:187`, `add_channel(h)` returns narrow delta REQs (one `#h`, one gstate) or empty if covered `:204`. [...] 1. **Relay subscriptions** ‚Äî the strongest fit and already half-built. `fabric/subscriptions.rs` is a pure `state ‚Üí Vec<PlannedReq>` planner returning open/close deltas ‚Äî exactly a plan. But: (a) it is applied via **two divergent paths** (incremental `add_channel` vs full `seed`) rather than one reconcile; (b) the **close/compact half is dead code** ‚Äî `SubscriptionRegistry::compact` (`subscriptions.rs:243`) and daemon `close_subs` (`daemon/server/subscriptions.rs:88`) are both `#[allow(dead_code)]`. Consequence: narrow REQs only ever accumulate; nothing ever unsubscribes a channel a session left. A `state ‚Üí {desired REQ set} ‚Üí diff against live ‚Üí open/close + receipt` engine would directly subsume this and fix the leak. [...] - **Teardown machinery is dead code**: `close_subs` (`subscriptions.rs:87-95`, verified) is `#[allow(dead_code)]`, doc'd *"Best-effort per id"*, zero non-test callers. `SubscriptionRegistry::compact` (`src/fabric/subscriptions.rs:243-244`) is likewise `#[allow(dead_code)]`: *"Not yet wired into the daemon ‚Äî compaction at a quiet boundary is a future optimization."* There is no `remove_channel`.
- **Self-documented history of this exact class of leak**: `src/transport.rs:293-298` ‚Äî *"random ids leaked an unbounded number of subscriptions into the relay pool ... growing the process to tens of GB over a day"* and `:369-374` ‚Äî *"The codebase had no unsubscribe path before ... The registry needs this to compact narrow REQs and retire stale subscriptions ... instead of letting the relay-side subscription set grow monotonically."*

**Verdict**: subscriptions structurally outlive the state that justified them for the life of the daemon process. Dedup (deterministic `SubscriptionId`, `transport.rs:290-298`) prevents *duplicate* leaks but there's no shrink/close path at all. [...] - **Full recompute exists but only runs once, at startup**: `resubscribe` is called from `lifecycle.rs:207`, inside the one-time startup sequence ‚Äî never from a leave/end RPC. [...] **Relevance to your reconciliation-engine idea**: the codebase already contains, verbatim, the exact primitive you're proposing (`resubscribe`/`build_entity_coverage` = derived-set recompute; `compact`/`close_subs` = diff-based children-first teardown) but wired to run only once, at startup, with the incremental live-path deliberately kept separate ("NOT a full aggregate rebuild," `subscriptions.rs:13`) for latency reasons on hook-critical paths. The gap is that nothing periodically (or on scope-close) re-runs that recompute-and-diff to retire subscriptions/membership/pending-inbox rows tied to channels/sessions that no longer exist ‚Äî which is precisely the drift class documented in `transport.rs:293-298` and structurally still open on the leave/session-end paths today. [...] ## 4. REFCOUNTING ‚Äî none exists

`grep -rn "refcount\|ref_count" src/` ‚Üí zero hits (confirmed pattern absent). Membership is tracked only via row-existence: `session_channels` (session‚Üíchannel rows) and `relay_channel_members` (NIP-29 mirror, per-pubkey). `build_entity_coverage` (`subscriptions.rs:107-158`) is the *only* function that scans all sessions to derive "who still needs this channel," and it's called solely from the startup-only `resubscribe` ‚Äî never on join/leave. So: shared subscriptions dedupe correctly on open (via deterministic IDs) but there is no shared-ownership counter and no close-on-last-leave ‚Äî the equivalent of a refcount that only ever increments, never decrements, and is only read once (at boot).
